author: @anthonyrepetto3474
text: A joy - thank you! :) The antidote to being a superstitious chimpanzee that I've found, which requires a sort of fortitude against our impulse to easy distraction, as well as the impulse to get-moving-and-done-quickly, is to give pause for the loose play and wandering moments, the odd findings that circle back into new insights... I remember when I found I could roll two quarters on-edge in a metal mixing bowl, generating an wonderful resonance! It didn't happen for the first twenty minutes... it was all cacophony until it hummed. And I was only trying to making the coins roll stably. Why? I'm still not sure. Those are the stray threads and dead ends that, out of a perversity of curiosity, we wander back to and check from another angle... to find new paths, sometimes.

replies.author: @anthonyrepetto3474
replies.text: Now, I'm imagining a brass bell with a bar spanning the opening, a pair of metal rollers on the tips of it - the beam and rollers spin on an axle attached to a frame in 'front' of the bell's mouth. Why? Beam and rollers could transfer a larger force into the walls of the bell, stably, up to resonance... and if I'm picturing it right, that's when the load on the axle will drop-off. You'll know because it gets even louder... and a conic-section shell housing the bell, for a beam of ultra-low frequency, generated by mechanical torque instead of electronic speakers and such? A sort of Dark Ages internet from an inverted singing bowl... or something else :)

author: @ThisRandomUsername
text:
Yeah, but you really can tell how much it's generating in real-time by just seeing how quickly the voltage rises and knowing the capacitance.
The formula is (capacitance) x (voltage rise) = (current) x (time). Do some cross division and you get current = (capacitance x voltage rise) / time.

replies.author: @ThisRandomUsername
replies.text: Rob, what's your plan when you start getting larger wind speed? Your capacitors will be charging too quickly to get a good reading from your meter? Maybe separate the capacitors with a switch and flip it for half a second?

author: @peterdkay
text:
I assume you hope to generate >10 Watts with your "1kW" generator.
Charging a capacitor generates 1/2 * V^2 * C joules of energy.  
Charging 1000 micro-Farads (0.1 Farads)  to 10 Volts has  0.05 joules of energy which is very low. 
However if capacitor is 1000 Farads, energy is 500 Joules which is respectable. If you can do it in 50 seconds you are generating 10 watts.

author: @NorthernKitty
text: Just thought I'd add one more quick observation.  I think our problems begin when we conflate "belief" with "knowledge".  The chimpanzees lacked any knowledge of the spray, how it worked and why it was there, and so instead developed a belief that the bananas were forbidden, which they then passed on to the new chimpanzees.  Beliefs are like theories - only handy for as long as it takes to develop knowledge.  We should be willing to test them repeatedly to gain knowledge, and to surrender them when knowledge contradicts them.

author: @OceanBagel
text:
I've heard this same parable discussed in the context of toxic traditions, or "That's the way we've always done it" when later generations should know better that the way it's always been done hurts people or is otherwise a destructive tradition. It's an entirely different context and meaning, but it's the same story of ingraining a certain behavior because past generations told you to.

In an engineering context, I'd say a good step to take is to learn to enjoy getting sprayed with cold water because it means you're learning things firsthand. A lot of big discoveries have come about because of people questioning the status quo. Of course things don't usually become the status quo without good reason, so a lot of times you'll just end up confirming what you were already told, but occasionally you break through into something that defies the status quo and that's the moment of discovery a lot of people seek.

author: @BensWorkshop
text:
It's true you can measure the starting and finishing voltage but that will tell you how much energy is generated over the time you can the experiment rather than power available but it does have limitations. You can't go over the voltage of the capacitor for one and the other is that you stop counting how much energy you are producing once the capacitors are charged as much as they can be by the voltage produced by the generator. 

Resistive loads will show you instant power and if it is a fluctuating generator as in the wind generator, you would have to monitor the voltage across the load over time and integrate to find the total energy output and divide by time to get power.

replies.author: @ThisRandomUsername
replies.text: You're right. You'd want to maintain wind speed in each run and start with empty capacitors, charging up to a certain voltage limit or until the voltage stops rising. I'd be inclined to measure the no-load RPM too for reference. The bigger issue here is when you start approaching a Watt of power you'd charge the capacitors too quickly for a multimeter to pick up, and you'd want an oscilloscope instead.

replies.author: @BensWorkshop
replies.text: @ThisRandomUsername You can with capacitors produce an average power out, by taking the energy accumulated and divide it by running time. The limitation is the amount of voltage that the unit can kick out and when the capacitors reach that they will stop charging so it is a long way from perfect. The other method also has draw backs.

replies.author: @ThisRandomUsername
replies.text:
@BensWorkshop Yeah, but if you have good enough resolution in time and voltage you can get pretty accurate results. The limit here would be the size of a suitable load capacitor at usable power levels.
Edit: With enough resolution you can can choose an arbitrary point on the capacitor charge curve to find its instantaneous power output. An oscilloscope would be most useful here.

replies.author: @BensWorkshop
replies.text: @ThisRandomUsername Yes, you can plot voltage against time and then work out from the change in voltage, the power however if you had that ability you could also use it with a resistive load to give the same result.

replies.author: @ThisRandomUsername
replies.text:
@BensWorkshop The thing I'm getting at is with a resistive load you won't be able to get a curve of voltage vs current at that specific wind speed, so you're not sure if you're just bogging the whole system. With the capacitors you can see that at 15 rpm, you get a certain number of microamps at 2 volts, and if you continue letting it charge up to say 4 volts, you might get 70% of that, or you might get 40% of it. Then you might turn up the leaf blower a bit to run the turbine at 30 rpm and get 100 microamps with a different maximum power point.

Edit: microvolts -> microamps

author: @totherarf
text:
Love the chimp story!
But I think you may be mistaken on the Caps.
A generator is measured in both Voltage and Power (or maybe Current (P=VA)) Charging your caps up will show the generator is outputting the voltage those caps hold  ..... but Not how much power the generator can give! .... Unless you have Really large caps a charge curve and a timepiece ;0)
Putting a decent load on with a volt meter and an ammeter is the best way ..... but it rarely matters that much!

author: @scantrain5007
text:
Dear Rob,
there is a lot of trues in the parable.
First of all: Research says: Animals cannot talk. This is correct. 
But ... in this parable is shown, that animals can communicate. In this case chimpanzees. In their own language of course. Might be gestcis, noise or whatever. Other animals like horses, dogs or cats can do simiar: If you live with them a bit closer together and observe them well you can find out.
The result is: Learn from each other. It makes sense and is well known. That's why we have scools, colleges and universities. 

Another way is ... to stay a bit like try and error. 
For example you know how a battery works and how is the construction. 
Step one is: You make one in the well known way. Take measurments about voltage, ampere output and so on. 

Step two: You go another way and change the electrolyte. Take measurements etc. Very often you will fail. But there is a chance you get  one which increase the performance.  

To the parable: 
If you don't try you will never find out and stay dry (and hungry). 
If you try you might get wet - but the chance to get the banana is there as well.

author: @paulcoover9197
text:
When you have a moment for reflection.....please swing by and see what is going on where I live. 
I have taken photographs of much interest to some scientists., but they are shadow banned. 
It's not your duty to "Believe"  or to "Disbelieve".
Don't discount Intel.
Later you will say, "I heard something more about that".
With enough info dovetailing as it were.  Laying into other info like scales on a fish......perfect.
 Then you have a Correlation which is very strong evidence to help you decide.
Point one.......We see these ___ often as the flash goes off. Two people see the same thing and is validated by the photograph taken. 
  These what? Nimbus, Halos, Spheres, plasma orbs, spheres of influence. Also called fake, said to be dust, lint, swamp gas.  Persons have said these are dust inside the optics. But why can we SEE them outside the camera as the flash fires?  It's an interesting story and the photon graphs are unstoppable.
If you have seen pictures so explicit of something new...or old......Please correct me.
@

author: @goldmagnet9013
text: Rob the trouble with the capacitor is it will only charge when the wind turbine voltage is higher than the cap, thus the higher the cap voltage the more power is missed. In the original video you were talking about output over a month ! To measure that you cant rely of discharging a cap you need a continuous measurement over time as the wind blows fast and slow, and you dont want to miss measuring that power that is below the cap voltage...   I am only quoting your words from what you set out to do. Merry Christmas to you and your team, cant wait to see how much power she harvests over a month..

replies.author: @1967spark
replies.text:
It's an interesting and complex situation. As you rightly say, when the turbine is slower and therefore it is generating a lower voltage than it had previously charged the capacitor to when the wind had been stronger.

According to Faraday “that a voltage is induced in a circuit whenever relative motion exists between a conductor and a magnetic field and that the magnitude of this voltage is proportional to the rate of change of the flux”.  (Unlike the chimpanzees we stand on the shoulders of giants)

BUT if we agree that there is no power being used to charge the capacitors then there is no power being removed from the turbine, via the coils,  allowing it to continue to spin freely, perhaps meaning that it won't need to overcome inertia to restart when the windspeed increases (though it is still subject to it's friction and other losses). The mass of the turbine then perhaps also assists as a flywheel?

Potentially it almost be acting as a minimum wind speed cut off, stopping power being drawn when the wind speed drops automatically.  However depending on how a load is eventually attached this effect could be lost if the capacitors were allowed to fully discharge.  However a circuit that prevents the capacitors from fully discharging would likely also work to stop power dissipation through a resistor when the generating voltage is low.

Given that this is now apparently an energy scavenging design to operate in low wind speeds the question really is which is more efficient in low variable wind speeds, extracting all the energy and effectively stopping the turbine, requiring it to overcome inertia again, or allowing it to spin freely at very low wind speeds?

There is therefore likely to be some differences in the output power measured, my instinct says measuring the capacitor charge is still likely to under estimate the power generated, but real world testing is probably the only way to know.

replies.author: @goldmagnet9013
replies.text: Rob in the initial video talks about power over time, so it makes sense to measure power over time, continuously. Anyone estimating their power consumption would do so as X watts in Y hours. Using a cap is a terrible idea, wont respond to being charged lower than its voltage, or indeed when its full. Maybe Rob will change when he has it in real world conditions and measures its output over a month, so people interested in the design can work out if its suitable for them.

replies.author: @1967spark
replies.text:
@goldmagnet9013 My instinct is to agree with you, but it's Rob's experiment. 

Regardless of whether he gets bananas or a soaking at the top of the ladder others will learn from it.

replies.author: @goldmagnet9013
replies.text: @1967spark Agreed. I guess is anyone wants to they can google "generator output measurement" and do it themselves. Still don't know how Robs going to conclude the project without measuring the power output continuously over time as per the intro video.
